key_insight: Raising argon flow from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying produces thicker, denser NiCrFeBSiC coatings with better wear behavior.
engineering_relevance: Process gas tuning is a practical lever to improve coating build quality, hardness-related performance, and tribological life in thermal spray applications.
method: Air plasma sprayed self-fluxing NiCrFeBSiC metal-ceramic coatings at two argon flow rates: 1600 and 1800 L/h.
Compared structure, porosity, microhardness, elastic modulus, adhesion, and ball-on-disk friction/wear behavior.
Used scratch testing to assess coating-substrate adhesion and failure stability.
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness increased by about 2×, porosity dropped by about 2×, and the friction coefficient decreased to approximately 0.107, with microhardness measured at 1015.8 ± 81.5 HV and elastic modulus at 203.9 ± 5.4 GPa.
watch_item: Results are based on only two argon flow settings, so broader process optimization is still needed before generalizing to other spray conditions.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
